High-Level Design: Design a Stock Trading Platform Like Zerodha
MEDIUM
role
Software Development Engineer 2
Round 3
High-Level Design
System Design (HLD)
exp
#143 · Mar 2024
● offer
System Design
High-Level Design (HLD)
High Availability
High Scalability

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
The objective is to design a high-level architecture for an online stock trading platform similar to Zerodha, which is one of the leading technology-driven stock trading platforms. This system should enable users to buy and sell stocks in real-time, view live market data

↗ Question on EngineBogie

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the matching/order flow, real-time market data fan-out, and consistency of the order book — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: this is a **money + real-time** system. Orders must never be lost or double-executed (strong consistency, like a bank ledger), but market data must reach millions of screens in milliseconds (massive read fan-out, eventual is fine). Those two halves want opposite architectures, so I'll split them.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/Uber-style bar** — front-load scale and the real-time hot path, lead with the state machine for an order. Tell me if it's more Amazon (cost/ops-heavy) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Place / modify / cancel an order** (market or limit, buy/sell) — the heart of the system.
- **Match orders & execute trades** against an order book, update holdings & funds.
- **Stream live market data** (last traded price, bid/ask, depth) to users in real time.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):**
- Portfolio analytics / P&L reports, KYC/onboarding, payments & bank settlement (T+1), margin/leverage engine, mutual funds, charting/historical candles, tax filing.

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Correctness above all** — no lost, duplicated, or double-filled orders. Strong consistency on the order path; an order is an immutable ledger event.
- **Availability** — order-entry gateway 99.99% during market hours; market-data path 99.9% (degrade gracefully).
- **Latency** — order ack p99 < 100 ms; match-to-execution inside the engine sub-millisecond; market-data tick fan-out p99 < 200 ms end-to-end.
- **Scale** — millions of users, ~hundreds of thousands of orders/sec at open, millions of concurrent market-data subscribers.
- **Durability** — every order/trade survives a node loss (RPO ≈ 0 on the order log).

> 💬 "The thing I keep front of mind: the write path is a bank, the read path is a stadium scoreboard. I'll design them separately."

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exchange matching (NSE/BSE) happens at the exchange, not at Zerodha — a broker mostly *routes* orders. I'll design the broker's order-management + a matching engine abstraction so the design stands whether we match in-house or forward to an exchange; I'll call out which is which.

> 🎙️ Script: "At its core people place buy and sell orders and expect them filled correctly and fast, while also watching live prices stream in. So I've got two very different jobs in one product. The order path is money — it has to be strongly consistent, durable, and never double-execute. The market-data path is a huge read fan-out where being a few hundred milliseconds stale is totally fine. I'll prioritize placing and matching orders, then live data. And I'll note up front that a real broker like Zerodha routes most orders to the NSE/BSE exchange rather than matching them itself — I'll design the order-management layer so it works either way."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each matters):**
- **Are we the exchange or a broker routing to NSE/BSE?** This decides whether we *own* the matching engine or just an order gateway + smart router. Hugely changes the core. → I'll assume **broker that owns a matching/OMS layer and routes to an external exchange**, so I cover both.
- **Order types in scope?** Market vs limit vs stop-loss vs bracket. → Assume **market + limit** for now, mention stop-loss as an extension.
- **Equities only, or F&O/commodities too?** → **Equities cash segment** only.
- **Do we hold funds/holdings ledger ourselves?** → Yes — wallet (funds) + demat holdings ledger are ours; bank settlement is out of scope.
- **Regulatory constraints?** Audit trail, immutability, replayability are mandated (SEBI). → Assume **every order event is immutable and auditable**.
- **Consistency expectation on market data?** → Eventual, low-latency; users tolerate ~100-200 ms staleness.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** equities, market+limit orders, we own OMS + funds/holdings ledger + a matching engine abstraction, external exchange for actual liquidity, immutable audit log mandatory, market hours 09:15–15:30 IST with a brutal spike at open.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Before I size it — are you happy treating us as a broker that owns the order-management and ledger but routes to the exchange? Or do you want me to build a full in-house matching engine? I'll design the engine either way, just want to know where to spend depth."

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that actually drive a decision — order QPS at open, market-data fan-out, and order-log storage.

content.text:
| Metric | Value | Say-it-out-loud |
|---|---|---|
| Total users / DAU | 10M / 2M | "ten million users, a couple million active daily" |
| Avg order rate | ~900/s | "under a thousand orders a second on average" |
| **Peak order rate at open** | ~13K/s | "**the open spike — call it 13K orders a second**" |
| Market-data raw fan-out | ~7.5M msgs/s | "millions of price-update pushes a second" |
| Order-event storage | ~22 GB/day, ~24 TB/yr (x3) | "tens of TB a year of immutable order log" |
| Tick history | ~28 GB/day | "~30 GB a day of market ticks" |

**The one number that forces the design: ~7.5M market-data messages/sec of fan-out vs only ~13K orders/sec.** That's a ~500:1 ratio. The flip: a single pub/sub node handles maybe ~100K pushes/s, so 7.5M means I *must* fan out through a tiered broadcast tree / edge layer, not a flat pub/sub. Meanwhile the order path is tiny in QPS but priceless in correctness — so I spend my durability budget there and my bandwidth budget on reads.

🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — the request rate a tier must absorb; sizing comes from peak, not average)*. **Fan-out** *(one event copied to many subscribers — the multiplier that blows up bandwidth)*.

> 🎙️ Script: "Order volume is actually modest — under a thousand a second normally, maybe thirteen thousand in the first five minutes when the market opens. The scary number is market data: roughly seven and a half million price pushes a second across a million-plus live screens. So this is a five-hundred-to-one read-to-write system. I'll make the order path bulletproof and cheap on throughput, and throw real engineering at broadcasting prices efficiently."

## 4. Core Entities

First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:
- **User / Account** — identity, linked funds wallet and demat holdings.
- **Order** — an intent to buy/sell N shares of a symbol at a price; has a lifecycle (NEW → OPEN → PARTIAL → FILLED/CANCELLED/REJECTED). Immutable events.
- **Trade / Execution** — a fill: matched buy+sell, price, qty, timestamp.
- **Instrument / Symbol** — a tradable stock (RELIANCE, INFY) with metadata.
- **Order Book** — per-symbol sorted bids and asks.
- **Holding** — shares a user owns (demat ledger).
- **Funds / Wallet** — cash balance, with reserved (blocked) amount for open orders.
- **Market Data Tick** — last price, bid/ask, depth snapshot per symbol.

> 🎙️ Script: "The key nouns are Users with a funds wallet and a holdings ledger, Orders that move through a strict lifecycle, Trades which are the fills, Instruments, the per-symbol Order Book, and Market-Data ticks. The Order and Trade are the ones I treat as immutable ledger events for audit. I'll firm up the exact fields in the data-model section."

## 5. API / Interface

REST/gRPC for commands, WebSocket for streaming. Caller identity always from the session/JWT — never trust a client-supplied userId, price, or balance.

**Place order** — serves "place an order":
```
POST /v1/orders
Header: Idempotency-Key: <uuid>
Body: { symbol, side: BUY|SELL, type: LIMIT|MARKET, qty, limitPrice? }
Resp: 202 { orderId, status: ACCEPTED }
```
POST creates a resource; **202 Accepted** because matching is async — we ack receipt, the fill comes via the stream. Idempotency-Key prevents a retried tap from placing two orders.

**Modify / Cancel:**
```
PUT    /v1/orders/{orderId}   { newQty?, newLimitPrice? }   -> 202
DELETE /v1/orders/{orderId}                                 -> 202
```

**Read order status & holdings:**
```
GET /v1/orders/{orderId}      -> order + fills
GET /v1/portfolio/holdings    -> positions, avg price
GET /v1/funds                 -> balance, blocked, available
```

**Stream market data + order updates (WebSocket):**
```
WS /v1/stream
  -> subscribe { symbols: [...] }
  <- tick { symbol, ltp, bid, ask, depth, ts }
  <- orderUpdate { orderId, status, filledQty, avgPrice }
```
One socket multiplexes price ticks *and* the user's own order/fill notifications — so a fill shows on screen the instant it happens.

> 💬 "Place is a POST that returns 202 not 200 — I'm acknowledging I received the order, not that it filled. The fill arrives on the WebSocket. That distinction is the whole async model."

> 🎙️ Script: "Placing an order is a POST with an idempotency key, and it returns 202 Accepted, not a fill — because matching is asynchronous and the execution comes back over a WebSocket. Modify and cancel are PUT and DELETE on the order id. Reads cover order status, holdings, and funds. And a single WebSocket carries both live price ticks and the user's own order updates, so fills appear instantly. Everywhere, I take the user id from the token, never the body — this is money, I trust nothing the client sends about identity or price."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Place / modify / cancel an order"

Start minimal: a client hits an **Order Gateway**, we validate funds, write the order as an immutable event, and acknowledge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Client app] -->|1 POST order| GW[Order Gateway]
  GW -->|2 check funds| RM[Risk and Funds svc]
  RM -->|3 reserve cash| LedgerDB[(Funds ledger)]
  GW -->|4 append order event| OQ[[Order log Kafka]]
  GW -->|5 202 accepted| C
  OQ -->|6 consume| OMS[OMS / Matching]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class GW,RM,LedgerDB,OQ,OMS cold;
```

1. Client POSTs the order (with Idempotency-Key).
2. Gateway calls **Risk & Funds** to validate: enough available cash for a buy / enough shares for a sell.
3. Funds service **reserves** (blocks) the cash atomically in the ledger — state change: available balance drops.
4. Gateway appends the order as an event to the **order log** — **Kafka** *(a durable, ordered, replayable event log; here it's the system of record for orders so nothing is lost and we can replay for audit/recovery)*.
5. Return **202** — order accepted, not yet matched.
6. The **OMS / matching engine** consumes the log downstream.

> 💬 "I reserve funds *before* accepting, so a user can never spend the same rupee twice. Then I write the order to a durable log — that log is my source of truth."

### 6.2 "Match orders & execute trades"

Now add the **Matching Engine**: a per-symbol, in-memory order book that matches buys against sells (or routes to the exchange), emits trades, and the trades update holdings/funds.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OQ[[Order log Kafka]] -->|1 ordered events| ME[Matching Engine per symbol]
  ME -->|2 match| OB[(In-mem order book)]
  ME -->|3 trade events| TQ[[Trade log Kafka]]
  ME -. 2b route .-> EX[External Exchange NSE/BSE]
  TQ -->|4 settle| SET[Settlement svc]
  SET -->|5a debit/credit| LedgerDB[(Funds ledger)]
  SET -->|5b update shares| HoldDB[(Holdings ledger)]
  TQ -->|6 notify| NOT[Notification svc]
  NOT -->|7 orderUpdate| WS[WebSocket layer]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class OQ,ME,OB,TQ,SET,LedgerDB,HoldDB cold;
  class NOT,WS hot;
```

1. Engine consumes orders **in log order** (per-symbol ordering = fairness).
2. Matches against the in-memory book (price-time priority); or **2b** routes to the external exchange and consumes its execution reports.
3. On a fill it emits **trade events** to the trade log.
4. **Settlement** consumes trades.
5. It **debits buyer cash / credits seller cash** (5a) and **moves shares** (5b) — converting reserved funds into a real transfer. State change: holdings & balances finalize.
6/7. Notification pushes an **orderUpdate** to the user's WebSocket — fill on screen.

> 💬 "The engine is single-writer per symbol and in-memory — that's how exchanges hit microseconds. The log feeding it gives me durability and exact replay."

### 6.3 "Stream live market data"

Add the read mega-path. The engine (or exchange feed) emits ticks; we fan them out through a broadcast tree to millions of sockets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  ME[Matching Engine] -->|1 tick events| MDQ[[Market-data bus]]
  EX[Exchange feed] -->|1b raw ticks| MDQ
  MDQ -->|2 publish| MD[Market-data svc]
  MD -->|3 conflate + cache| RT[Redis last-price]
  MD -->|4 fan-out| EDGE[Edge / WS gateway fleet]
  EDGE -->|5 push ticks| C[Millions of clients]
  C -->|0 subscribe symbols| EDGE
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class MDQ,MD,RT,EDGE,C hot;
```

1. Ticks flow from the engine/exchange onto the **market-data bus**.
2. **Market-data service** consumes them.
3. It **conflates** (collapses bursts to the latest value per symbol) and caches last price in **Redis** *(in-memory key-value store; here it serves the latest price instantly to new subscribers and reconnects without replaying the bus)*.
4. It fans out to a fleet of **edge WebSocket gateways**, each owning a subset of connections.
5. Each edge node pushes only the symbols its connected users subscribed to.

> 💬 "I conflate aggressively — if a price ticks 50 times in a second, a human only needs the latest. That single trick cuts the fan-out dramatically."

**Final (high-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Clients] -->|orders REST| GW[Order Gateway]
  C <-->|WS ticks + fills| EDGE[Edge WS fleet]
  GW --> RM[Risk and Funds]
  RM --> LedgerDB[(Funds ledger)]
  GW --> OQ[[Order log]]
  OQ --> ME[Matching Engine - book in mem - price-time priority]
  ME -.-> EX[Exchange]
  ME --> TQ[[Trade log]]
  ME --> MDQ[[Market-data bus]]
  TQ --> SET[Settlement]
  SET --> LedgerDB
  SET --> HoldDB[(Holdings ledger - userId+symbol)]
  TQ --> NOT[Notification]
  NOT --> EDGE
  MDQ --> MD[Market-data svc]
  MD --> RT[(Redis last price)]
  MD --> EDGE
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class C,EDGE,MDQ,MD,RT,NOT hot;
  class GW,RM,LedgerDB,OQ,ME,TQ,SET,HoldDB cold;
```

> 🎙️ Script: "End to end: an order comes into the gateway, we reserve funds, append it to a durable ordered log, and ack with a 202. A per-symbol matching engine consumes that log in order, matches in memory or routes to the exchange, and emits trades to a trade log. Settlement turns those trades into real cash and share movements in the ledgers, and a notification rides the user's WebSocket so the fill shows instantly. Separately, every price tick flows onto a market-data bus, gets conflated and cached in Redis, and fans out through an edge WebSocket fleet to millions of screens. Two paths: a durable, ordered, single-writer money path in amber, and a high-fan-out read path in green."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton. The two richest deep dives are the matching-engine consistency/ordering, and the market-data fan-out at 7.5M/s. Which do you want first — or are you more interested in the funds-reservation correctness?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields | Store + why |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | orderId, userId, symbol, side, type, qty, limitPrice, status, filledQty, ts | **Append-only log (Kafka) + indexed DB (Postgres/Cassandra) for query.** Immutable, ordered, auditable. Partition by symbol on the log (ordering), by userId in query DB. |
| **Trade** | tradeId, buyOrderId, sellOrderId, symbol, price, qty, ts | Append-only log + OLAP store. Immutable audit record. |
| **Funds ledger** | userId, available, reserved, version | **Strongly consistent SQL (Postgres)** — money needs ACID transactions + row-level locks. Sharded by userId. |
| **Holdings** | userId, symbol, qty, avgPrice | **SQL**, sharded by userId. Strong consistency on writes. |
| **Order book** | symbol → sorted bids/asks | **In-memory** in the matching engine (rebuilt from log on restart). |
| **Last price / depth** | symbol → ltp, bid, ask, depth | **Redis** — ephemeral, eventual, microsecond reads for new subscribers. |
| **Tick history** | symbol, ts, ohlc | **Time-series DB (e.g. ClickHouse)** for charts — out of core scope. |

🧠 **ACID** *(transaction guarantees — all-or-nothing, isolated; here it stops two orders spending the same cash)*. **OLAP** *(analytics-optimized columnar store; here for trade history and reporting, separate from the hot path)*.

> 🎙️ Script: "Money lives in strongly-consistent SQL — funds and holdings ledgers, sharded by user, with real ACID transactions because we can't double-spend. Orders and trades are append-only immutable logs for durability and audit, indexed into a query DB for lookups. The order book itself lives in memory in the engine and is rebuilt by replaying the log on restart. And last prices live in Redis — ephemeral and eventually consistent because being a beat stale on a price is harmless."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "money path = strong + ordered + durable, read path = fan-out + eventual" and reason forward from there.

### How do we match orders correctly, fast, and without double-execution?

**#### Bad: stateless matching workers hitting a shared SQL order book**
**Approach** — N workers read open orders from a SQL table, match, write trades.
**Challenges** — race conditions: two workers match the same resting order → **double-execution** (we sell 100 shares twice). Locking the book per match serializes everything and tanks throughput. ⚠️ **Trap:** treating the order book as ordinary CRUD data — under the open spike this both corrupts and crawls.

**#### Good: single-writer in-memory engine per symbol, fed by a log**
**Approach** — One engine instance owns each symbol's book entirely in memory; it consumes a **per-symbol-partitioned Kafka log** so events arrive in a total order. Match with **price-time priority**. Emit trades to a trade log. No locks — one thread, one symbol.
**Challenges** — if the engine crashes, the in-memory book is gone, and a single instance is a SPOF.

**#### Great: single-writer + log replay + hot-standby replicas**
**Approach** — The log *is* the source of truth, so on crash a standby replays the log from the last snapshot and rebuilds the exact book — deterministic because input order is fixed. Run a **hot standby** consuming the same partition; on failure it's already warm and takes over (leader election via the consumer group / a coordination service). Periodic **snapshots** bound replay time. Symbols are sharded across many engine instances, so we scale horizontally and a hot symbol (RELIANCE at open) gets its own dedicated instance.
**Trade-offs/why real systems do this** — this is exactly the LMAX/exchange pattern: determinism + an ordered log gives you correctness *and* microsecond matching *and* free audit/replay. Cost: failover takes seconds (replay), so we keep snapshots frequent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OQ[[Order log partition=symbol]] -->|ordered| L[Leader engine RELIANCE]
  OQ -->|same stream| S[Hot standby RELIANCE]
  L -->|snapshot| SNAP[(Snapshot store)]
  S -.->|replay on failover| SNAP
  L -->|trades| TQ[[Trade log]]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class OQ,L,S,SNAP,TQ cold;
```

**Bottleneck:** a single engine thread does maybe ~1M matches/s — way above our 13K/s peak even for one hot symbol, so the binding limit is actually log throughput and settlement, not matching. New ceiling: Kafka partition ~ hundreds of MB/s, fine.

> 🎙️ Script: "I make matching a single-writer, in-memory engine per symbol, fed by an ordered durable log. One thread per symbol means no locks and no double-execution — order is decided by the log. Because matching is deterministic on that input order, I can crash and rebuild the exact book by replaying from a snapshot, and I keep a hot standby already consuming the same stream for fast failover. That's the LMAX/exchange pattern — correctness, microsecond speed, and a free audit trail all at once."

> 🧠 If they ask "why not just lock rows in SQL?": "Locking serializes the whole book and still risks double-fills under contention. Single-writer-per-symbol removes the race by construction — there's only one writer, so there's nothing to lock."

### How do we fan out 7.5M ticks/sec to millions of clients?

**#### Bad: clients poll GET /price every second**
**Approach** — clients poll. **Challenges** — 1.5M clients × 1/s = 1.5M QPS of HTTP overhead, terrible latency, and still stale. Dead on arrival.

**#### Good: flat pub/sub — every client subscribes to a topic**
**Approach** — Redis/Kafka pub/sub per symbol; clients subscribe. **Challenges** — a single broker node caps ~100K pushes/s; 7.5M needs a coordinated tree, and one popular symbol concentrates load on one node (hot key).

**#### Great: tiered broadcast tree + conflation + edge gateways**
**Approach** — (1) **Conflate** at the source: collapse N ticks/sec per symbol to a fixed publish rate (say 4-10/s) — humans can't see more. That alone cuts raw volume massively. (2) A **broadcast tree**: market-data service → regional relays → edge WebSocket gateways, each gateway owning ~50-100K connections and subscribing upstream *once* per symbol regardless of how many of its clients want it (de-duplicated fan-out). (3) New subscribers get the current value instantly from **Redis last-price** so they don't wait for the next tick.
**Trade-offs** — eventual consistency (a few hundred ms stale, acceptable); hot symbols still concentrate at the relay layer but the tree spreads the multiplication across many edge nodes instead of one.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  ME[Engine/Exchange feed] --> MD[Market-data svc - conflate]
  MD --> RT[(Redis last price)]
  MD --> R1[Relay region A]
  MD --> R2[Relay region B]
  R1 --> E1[Edge 50k conns]
  R1 --> E2[Edge 50k conns]
  R2 --> E3[Edge 50k conns]
  E1 --> CL[Clients]
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class ME,MD,RT,R1,R2,E1,E2,E3,CL hot;
```

**Math:** 1.5M conns ÷ 50K per edge = **~30 edge nodes**. Each symbol is subscribed once per edge node, so upstream from edges = 30 × 5000 symbols × ~5/s ≈ 750K msgs/s spread across relays — manageable, vs 7.5M flat.

> 🎙️ Script: "The trick is don't actually send 7.5 million messages. First I conflate — a price that ticks fifty times a second gets published a handful of times, because a human can't see more. Then I build a broadcast tree: the market-data service feeds regional relays which feed edge WebSocket gateways, each owning maybe fifty thousand connections and subscribing to each symbol just once no matter how many of its users want it. New subscribers get the latest price straight from Redis so they're not waiting. That turns a flat 7.5 million into a few hundred thousand spread across thirty-ish edge nodes."

> 🧠 If they ask "why WebSocket not Server-Sent-Events or polling?": "We need server-push at low latency and the client also sends subscribe messages, so a bidirectional WebSocket fits; SSE is one-way and polling is too slow and wasteful at this fan-out."

### How do we guarantee funds aren't double-spent (idempotency + reservation)?

**#### Great: reserve-on-accept + idempotency key + ACID ledger**
**Approach** — Before accepting an order we run an ACID transaction: `available -= cost; reserved += cost` with an optimistic **version** check. The **Idempotency-Key** is stored uniquely; a retry returns the original orderId instead of re-reserving. On fill, settlement converts reserved→spent; on cancel/reject, reserved→available. Every step is a ledger event.
**Read-your-own-writes:** after the 202, the client's funds query reads from the **primary** (or carries a version token) so the user always sees the freshly-blocked balance, never a lagging replica.
**Namespace collision:** orderIds are generated server-side (Snowflake-style: timestamp+shard+seq), so no two gateways can mint the same id; the idempotency key dedups client retries.

> 🧠 If they ask "what if settlement double-processes a trade?": "Each trade has a unique id and settlement is idempotent — applying the same tradeId twice is a no-op via a processed-set with the ledger update in one transaction."

> 🎙️ Script: "Money is never spent twice because I reserve cash in an ACID transaction at accept-time, with a version check, and an idempotency key so a retried tap returns the same order instead of double-reserving. The fill just converts reserved to spent; a cancel returns it. And right after placing, the user's balance read goes to the primary so they always see their own blocked funds immediately."

### Global coordinators
- **Per-symbol log partition leader** — ceiling: one writer; failover via consumer-group leader election + log replay (seconds). If down, that symbol halts (correct — better than mismatching).
- **Funds ledger shard primary** — ACID writes; multi-AZ sync replica, failover promotes replica (RPO 0, RTO seconds).
- **Snowflake ID generator** — per-node, no central coordinator, so no SPOF.

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

- **Availability:** order gateway 99.99% (four 9s) during market hours; market-data 99.9%.
- **Graceful degradation:** if market-data fails → order path still works (you can trade blind, prices freeze with a "stale" banner). If matching for a symbol fails → reject new orders on *that symbol* only, others trade. If Redis last-price down → new subscribers wait for next live tick.
- **RPO/RTO:** order & trade logs and funds ledger = **RPO 0** (synchronous replication, multi-AZ), RTO seconds (replay + promote). Market-data/Redis = RPO doesn't matter (ephemeral), rebuild from bus. Tested restore: nightly replay of the order log into a staging book and reconcile against settlement.
- **Multi-AZ vs region:** synchronous across AZs in one region for the money path (latency-sensitive matching can't afford cross-region sync); async DR replica in a second region with documented failover for disaster.
- **Cost driver:** the dominant cost is the **edge WebSocket fleet + egress bandwidth** for market data (millions of persistent connections), not the DB. Rough order: tens of edge nodes + bandwidth ≫ the modest order DB. I'd ballpark the market-data tier as the majority of monthly infra spend.
- **Ops:** SLO burn-rate alerts on order-ack p99 and fill latency, distributed tracing order→trade→settlement, runbooks for "symbol engine down" and "exchange feed gap."

> 🎙️ Script: "I run the money path synchronously across availability zones with zero data loss and seconds to fail over by replaying the log. The market-data path is allowed to fail soft — prices freeze with a stale banner but you can still trade. The biggest cost isn't the database, it's holding millions of WebSocket connections and the egress to push prices, so that's where I'd optimize spend with conflation and efficient binary framing."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **In-house matching vs route-to-exchange.** I designed an owned engine for depth, but a real Indian broker routes to NSE/BSE. Gave up: actual liquidity/regulatory reality. Reverses if: we *are* the exchange or run an internal dark pool. I'd confirm with the interviewer which world we're in.
- **Conflation of market data.** Sacrifices tick-by-tick fidelity for ~500x less fan-out. Reverses for: algo/HFT clients who need every tick — they'd get a separate raw, co-located feed, not the retail WebSocket.
- **Strong consistency on the whole money path.** Costs latency and caps us to single-region sync. Reverses only if: we accept eventual settlement (we won't — it's money).

> 🎙️ Script: "The two calls I'd most want to confirm: whether we own matching or route to the exchange, since that reshapes the core; and conflation, which is perfect for retail but wrong for algo traders who'd need a separate raw feed. The one thing I won't bend is strong consistency on funds — it's money, eventual isn't an option."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. How do you guarantee order fairness?**
Per-symbol orders go into a single Kafka partition, giving a total order, and the engine matches strictly price-time priority. Because one thread owns the symbol, there's no race to reorder. The log timestamp is the tiebreaker and it's auditable.
> 💬 "One partition per symbol gives total ordering; price-time priority on a single writer means fairness is guaranteed by construction."

**2. What happens at market open when 13K orders/sec hit?**
Orders are absorbed by the durable log instantly (Kafka handles far more), so we never drop them; the engine drains at its own pace and acks are 202 so users aren't blocked. Hot symbols get dedicated engine instances pre-provisioned for open. Risk checks are the likely bottleneck, so we pre-warm funds-ledger connections and cache balances.
> 💬 "The log is a shock absorber — we accept everything durably and match as fast as we can; nobody's order is lost."

**3. How do you prevent a user spending the same money twice?**
An ACID transaction reserves funds at accept-time with an optimistic version check before the order is logged. The idempotency key dedups retries. Fills convert reserved to spent atomically.
> 💬 "We block the cash before we accept the order, in one ACID transaction — there's no window to double-spend."

**4. Engine crashes mid-session — what happens?**
The in-memory book is reconstructable: a hot standby replays from the last snapshot plus subsequent log events, deterministically rebuilding the identical book, then takes over leadership. In-flight orders aren't lost because they're in the log, not in the engine.
> 💬 "Nothing's lost — the log is truth, the standby replays it and rebuilds the exact book in seconds."

**5. How do you stream to millions without melting?**
Conflate at source, then fan out through a tree of relays and edge gateways where each gateway subscribes once per symbol. New clients seed from Redis. That converts millions of raw ticks into a few hundred K of de-duplicated pushes.
> 💬 "I don't send every tick to everyone — I conflate and fan out through a tree, subscribing once per symbol per edge."

**6. Strong vs eventual consistency — where each?**
Funds, holdings, order state = strong (ACID, read-from-primary after write). Market data, last price = eventual (Redis, a few hundred ms stale is fine). The split is by whether staleness can cause financial harm.
> 💬 "Money is strong, prices are eventual — I draw the line at financial harm."

**7. How do you handle a stop-loss order?**
Stop orders rest in a separate trigger store keyed by symbol; the market-data stream feeds a trigger evaluator that, when last price crosses the stop, injects a market/limit order into the normal log. So it reuses the whole pipeline — only the trigger is new.
> 💬 "A stop-loss is a parked trigger; when price crosses, we inject a normal order into the same flow."

**8. What if the exchange feed has a gap or goes down?**
We detect via sequence-number gaps in the feed, mark affected symbols stale, halt new orders on them, and request a snapshot/recovery feed from the exchange. We never match on stale data.
> 💬 "Sequence gaps trigger a halt-and-resync on those symbols — better to pause than trade on bad prices."

**9. How do you audit / replay for regulators?**
Every order and trade is an immutable, ordered, timestamped log event retained for the mandated period; we can replay any window deterministically to reproduce exact state. The query DB is just a materialized view of the log.
> 💬 "The log is the immutable system of record — we can replay any moment exactly for SEBI."

**10. How do you shard, and what's the hot-shard risk?**
Engine sharded by symbol, ledgers by userId. Hot risk is a single popular symbol at open — mitigated by giving high-volume symbols dedicated engine instances and ensuring matching throughput (~1M/s) dwarfs even spiked single-symbol load.
> 💬 "Shard by symbol for matching, by user for money; hot symbols get their own dedicated engine."

**11. Why 202 and not 200 on order placement?**
Matching is asynchronous, so a synchronous 200 with a fill would force us to block the request on the engine and couple the gateway to matching latency. 202 means accepted-and-durable; the fill arrives on the WebSocket.
> 💬 "202 says 'I've durably got your order,' not 'it filled' — fills come async on the socket."

**12. How would you add charting / historical candles later?**
Tap the trade and tick logs into a time-series/OLAP store like ClickHouse, pre-aggregate into 1m/5m/1d candles, serve via a separate read API and CDN. It's a downstream consumer — zero impact on the hot paths.
> 💬 "Charts are just another consumer of the same logs into a time-series DB — fully decoupled."

**13. Network drops mid-WebSocket — does the user miss a fill?**
Order updates are also persisted; on reconnect the client fetches order/fill state via GET and resubscribes, so the WebSocket is a fast-path, not the only path. No fill is ever known only on the wire.
> 💬 "The socket is a convenience — fills are durable, so on reconnect we just re-fetch and re-sync."

**14. Multi-region active-active for the money path?**
No — the money path is single-write-region with synchronous multi-AZ replication, because cross-region sync would add tens of ms to matching and risk split-brain on the ledger. Second region is warm DR with async replication and a documented failover.
> 💬 "Money stays single-region synchronous; cross-region is DR, not active-active — split-brain on a ledger is unacceptable."

---

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is really two systems. The money path: orders come into a gateway, we reserve funds in an ACID transaction, append the order to a durable per-symbol ordered log, and ack with a 202. A single-writer in-memory matching engine per symbol consumes that log, matches with price-time priority or routes to the exchange, and emits trades that settlement turns into real cash and share movements — all strongly consistent and fully replayable for audit, with a hot standby that rebuilds the book from the log on failure. The read path is a 500-to-1 fan-out problem: 7.5 million price ticks a second to a million-plus screens. I solve it by conflating ticks at the source and fanning out through a tree of relays and edge WebSocket gateways, each subscribing once per symbol, with Redis serving the latest price to new subscribers. Money is strong and single-region synchronous; prices are eventual and degrade gracefully. The dominant cost is the WebSocket edge fleet, not the database."

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the matching/order flow, real-time market data fan-out, and consistency of the order book — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: this is a **money + real-time** system. Orders must never be lost or double-executed (strong consistency, like a bank ledger), but market data must reach millions of screens in milliseconds (massive read fan-out, eventual is fine). Those two halves want opposite architectures, so I'll split them.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/Uber-style bar** — front-load scale and the real-time hot path, lead with the state machine for an order. Tell me if it's more Amazon (cost/ops-heavy) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Place / modify / cancel an order** (market or limit, buy/sell) — the heart of the system.
- **Match orders & execute trades** against an order book, update holdings & funds.
- **Stream live market data** (last traded price, bid/ask, depth) to users in real time.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):**
- Portfolio analytics / P&L reports, KYC/onboarding, payments & bank settlement (T+1), margin/leverage engine, mutual funds, charting/historical candles, tax filing.

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Correctness above all** — no lost, duplicated, or double-filled orders. Strong consistency on the order path; an order is an immutable ledger event.
- **Availability** — order-entry gateway 99.99% during market hours; market-data path 99.9% (degrade gracefully).
- **Latency** — order ack p99 < 100 ms; match-to-execution inside the engine sub-millisecond; market-data tick fan-out p99 < 200 ms end-to-end.
- **Scale** — millions of users, ~hundreds of thousands of orders/sec at open, millions of concurrent market-data subscribers.
- **Durability** — every order/trade survives a node loss (RPO ≈ 0 on the order log).

> 💬 "The thing I keep front of mind: the write path is a bank, the read path is a stadium scoreboard. I'll design them separately."

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exchange matching (NSE/BSE) happens at the exchange, not at Zerodha — a broker mostly *routes* orders. I'll design the broker's order-management + a matching engine abstraction so the design stands whether we match in-house or forward to an exchange; I'll call out which is which.

> 🎙️ Script: "At its core people place buy and sell orders and expect them filled correctly and fast, while also watching live prices stream in. So I've got two very different jobs in one product. The order path is money — it has to be strongly consistent, durable, and never double-execute. The market-data path is a huge read fan-out where being a few hundred milliseconds stale is totally fine. I'll prioritize placing and matching orders, then live data. And I'll note up front that a real broker like Zerodha routes most orders to the NSE/BSE exchange rather than matching them itself — I'll design the order-management layer so it works either way."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each matters):**
- **Are we the exchange or a broker routing to NSE/BSE?** This decides whether we *own* the matching engine or just an order gateway + smart router. Hugely changes the core. → I'll assume **broker that owns a matching/OMS layer and routes to an external exchange**, so I cover both.
- **Order types in scope?** Market vs limit vs stop-loss vs bracket. → Assume **market + limit** for now, mention stop-loss as an extension.
- **Equities only, or F&O/commodities too?** → **Equities cash segment** only.
- **Do we hold funds/holdings ledger ourselves?** → Yes — wallet (funds) + demat holdings ledger are ours; bank settlement is out of scope.
- **Regulatory constraints?** Audit trail, immutability, replayability are mandated (SEBI). → Assume **every order event is immutable and auditable**.
- **Consistency expectation on market data?** → Eventual, low-latency; users tolerate ~100-200 ms staleness.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** equities, market+limit orders, we own OMS + funds/holdings ledger + a matching engine abstraction, external exchange for actual liquidity, immutable audit log mandatory, market hours 09:15–15:30 IST with a brutal spike at open.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Before I size it — are you happy treating us as a broker that owns the order-management and ledger but routes to the exchange? Or do you want me to build a full in-house matching engine? I'll design the engine either way, just want to know where to spend depth."

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that actually drive a decision — order QPS at open, market-data fan-out, and order-log storage.

| Metric | Value | Say-it-out-loud |
|---|---|---|
| Total users / DAU | 10M / 2M | "ten million users, a couple million active daily" |
| Avg order rate | ~900/s | "under a thousand orders a second on average" |
| **Peak order rate at open** | ~13K/s | "**the open spike — call it 13K orders a second**" |
| Market-data raw fan-out | ~7.5M msgs/s | "millions of price-update pushes a second" |
| Order-event storage | ~22 GB/day, ~24 TB/yr (x3) | "tens of TB a year of immutable order log" |
| Tick history | ~28 GB/day | "~30 GB a day of market ticks" |

**The one number that forces the design: ~7.5M market-data messages/sec of fan-out vs only ~13K orders/sec.** That's a ~500:1 ratio. The flip: a single pub/sub node handles maybe ~100K pushes/s, so 7.5M means I *must* fan out through a tiered broadcast tree / edge layer, not a flat pub/sub. Meanwhile the order path is tiny in QPS but priceless in correctness — so I spend my durability budget there and my bandwidth budget on reads.

🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — the request rate a tier must absorb; sizing comes from peak, not average)*. **Fan-out** *(one event copied to many subscribers — the multiplier that blows up bandwidth)*.

> 🎙️ Script: "Order volume is actually modest — under a thousand a second normally, maybe thirteen thousand in the first five minutes when the market opens. The scary number is market data: roughly seven and a half million price pushes a second across a million-plus live screens. So this is a five-hundred-to-one read-to-write system. I'll make the order path bulletproof and cheap on throughput, and throw real engineering at broadcasting prices efficiently."

## 4. Core Entities

First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:
- **User / Account** — identity, linked funds wallet and demat holdings.
- **Order** — an intent to buy/sell N shares of a symbol at a price; has a lifecycle (NEW → OPEN → PARTIAL → FILLED/CANCELLED/REJECTED). Immutable events.
- **Trade / Execution** — a fill: matched buy+sell, price, qty, timestamp.
- **Instrument / Symbol** — a tradable stock (RELIANCE, INFY) with metadata.
- **Order Book** — per-symbol sorted bids and asks.
- **Holding** — shares a user owns (demat ledger).
- **Funds / Wallet** — cash balance, with reserved (blocked) amount for open orders.
- **Market Data Tick** — last price, bid/ask, depth snapshot per symbol.

> 🎙️ Script: "The key nouns are Users with a funds wallet and a holdings ledger, Orders that move through a strict lifecycle, Trades which are the fills, Instruments, the per-symbol Order Book, and Market-Data ticks. The Order and Trade are the ones I treat as immutable ledger events for audit. I'll firm up the exact fields in the data-model section."

## 5. API / Interface

REST/gRPC for commands, WebSocket for streaming. Caller identity always from the session/JWT — never trust a client-supplied userId, price, or balance.

**Place order** — serves "place an order":
```
POST /v1/orders
Header: Idempotency-Key: <uuid>
Body: { symbol, side: BUY|SELL, type: LIMIT|MARKET, qty, limitPrice? }
Resp: 202 { orderId, status: ACCEPTED }
```
POST creates a resource; **202 Accepted** because matching is async — we ack receipt, the fill comes via the stream. Idempotency-Key prevents a retried tap from placing two orders.

**Modify / Cancel:**
```
PUT    /v1/orders/{orderId}   { newQty?, newLimitPrice? }   -> 202
DELETE /v1/orders/{orderId}                                 -> 202
```

**Read order status & holdings:**
```
GET /v1/orders/{orderId}      -> order + fills
GET /v1/portfolio/holdings    -> positions, avg price
GET /v1/funds                 -> balance, blocked, available
```

**Stream market data + order updates (WebSocket):**
```
WS /v1/stream
  -> subscribe { symbols: [...] }
  <- tick { symbol, ltp, bid, ask, depth, ts }
  <- orderUpdate { orderId, status, filledQty, avgPrice }
```
One socket multiplexes price ticks *and* the user's own order/fill notifications — so a fill shows on screen the instant it happens.

> 💬 "Place is a POST that returns 202 not 200 — I'm acknowledging I received the order, not that it filled. The fill arrives on the WebSocket. That distinction is the whole async model."

> 🎙️ Script: "Placing an order is a POST with an idempotency key, and it returns 202 Accepted, not a fill — because matching is asynchronous and the execution comes back over a WebSocket. Modify and cancel are PUT and DELETE on the order id. Reads cover order status, holdings, and funds. And a single WebSocket carries both live price ticks and the user's own order updates, so fills appear instantly. Everywhere, I take the user id from the token, never the body — this is money, I trust nothing the client sends about identity or price."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Place / modify / cancel an order"

Start minimal: a client hits an **Order Gateway**, we validate funds, write the order as an immutable event, and acknowledge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Client app] -->|1 POST order| GW[Order Gateway]
  GW -->|2 check funds| RM[Risk and Funds svc]
  RM -->|3 reserve cash| LedgerDB[(Funds ledger)]
  GW -->|4 append order event| OQ[[Order log Kafka]]
  GW -->|5 202 accepted| C
  OQ -->|6 consume| OMS[OMS / Matching]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class GW,RM,LedgerDB,OQ,OMS cold;
```

1. Client POSTs the order (with Idempotency-Key).
2. Gateway calls **Risk & Funds** to validate: enough available cash for a buy / enough shares for a sell.
3. Funds service **reserves** (blocks) the cash atomically in the ledger — state change: available balance drops.
4. Gateway appends the order as an event to the **order log** — **Kafka** *(a durable, ordered, replayable event log; here it's the system of record for orders so nothing is lost and we can replay for audit/recovery)*.
5. Return **202** — order accepted, not yet matched.
6. The **OMS / matching engine** consumes the log downstream.

> 💬 "I reserve funds *before* accepting, so a user can never spend the same rupee twice. Then I write the order to a durable log — that log is my source of truth."

### 6.2 "Match orders & execute trades"

Now add the **Matching Engine**: a per-symbol, in-memory order book that matches buys against sells (or routes to the exchange), emits trades, and the trades update holdings/funds.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OQ[[Order log Kafka]] -->|1 ordered events| ME[Matching Engine per symbol]
  ME -->|2 match| OB[(In-mem order book)]
  ME -->|3 trade events| TQ[[Trade log Kafka]]
  ME -. 2b route .-> EX[External Exchange NSE/BSE]
  TQ -->|4 settle| SET[Settlement svc]
  SET -->|5a debit/credit| LedgerDB[(Funds ledger)]
  SET -->|5b update shares| HoldDB[(Holdings ledger)]
  TQ -->|6 notify| NOT[Notification svc]
  NOT -->|7 orderUpdate| WS[WebSocket layer]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class OQ,ME,OB,TQ,SET,LedgerDB,HoldDB cold;
  class NOT,WS hot;
```

1. Engine consumes orders **in log order** (per-symbol ordering = fairness).
2. Matches against the in-memory book (price-time priority); or **2b** routes to the external exchange and consumes its execution reports.
3. On a fill it emits **trade events** to the trade log.
4. **Settlement** consumes trades.
5. It **debits buyer cash / credits seller cash** (5a) and **moves shares** (5b) — converting reserved funds into a real transfer. State change: holdings & balances finalize.
6/7. Notification pushes an **orderUpdate** to the user's WebSocket — fill on screen.

> 💬 "The engine is single-writer per symbol and in-memory — that's how exchanges hit microseconds. The log feeding it gives me durability and exact replay."

### 6.3 "Stream live market data"

Add the read mega-path. The engine (or exchange feed) emits ticks; we fan them out through a broadcast tree to millions of sockets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  ME[Matching Engine] -->|1 tick events| MDQ[[Market-data bus]]
  EX[Exchange feed] -->|1b raw ticks| MDQ
  MDQ -->|2 publish| MD[Market-data svc]
  MD -->|3 conflate + cache| RT[Redis last-price]
  MD -->|4 fan-out| EDGE[Edge / WS gateway fleet]
  EDGE -->|5 push ticks| C[Millions of clients]

  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class MDQ,MD,RT,EDGE,C hot;
```

1. Ticks flow from the engine/exchange onto the **market-data bus**.
2. **Market-data service** consumes them.
3. It **conflates** (collapses bursts to the latest value per symbol) and caches last price in **Redis** *(in-memory key-value store; here it serves the latest price instantly to new subscribers and reconnects without replaying the bus)*.
4. It fans out to a fleet of **edge WebSocket gateways**, each owning a subset of connections.
5. Each edge node pushes only the symbols its connected users subscribed to.

> 💬 "I conflate aggressively — if a price ticks 50 times in a second, a human only needs the latest. That single trick cuts the fan-out dramatically."

> 🎙️ Script: "End to end: an order comes into the gateway, we reserve funds, append it to a durable ordered log, and ack with a 202. A per-symbol matching engine consumes that log in order, matches in memory or routes to the exchange, and emits trades to a trade log. Separately, every price tick flows onto a market-data bus, gets conflated and cached in Redis, and fans out through an edge WebSocket fleet to millions of screens. ## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields | Store + why |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | orderId, userId, symbol, side, type, qty, limitPrice, status, filledQty, ts | **Append-only log (Kafka) + indexed DB (Postgres/Cassandra) for query.** Immutable, ordered, auditable. Partition by symbol on the log (ordering), by userId in query DB. |
| **Trade** | tradeId, buyOrderId, sellOrderId, symbol, price, qty, ts | Append-only log + OLAP store. Immutable audit record. |
| **Funds ledger** | userId, available, reserved, version | **Strongly consistent SQL (Postgres)** — money needs ACID transactions + row-level locks. Sharded by userId. |
| **Holdings** | userId, symbol, qty, avgPrice | **SQL**, sharded by userId. Strong consistency on writes. |
| **Order book** | symbol → sorted bids/asks | **In-memory** in the matching engine (rebuilt from log on restart). |
| **Last price / depth** | symbol → ltp, bid, ask, depth | **Redis** — ephemeral, eventual, microsecond reads for new subscribers. |
| **Tick history** | symbol, ts, ohlc | **Time-series DB (e.g. ClickHouse)** for charts — out of core scope. |

🧠 **ACID** *(transaction guarantees — all-or-nothing, isolated; here it stops two orders spending the same cash)*. **OLAP** *(analytics-optimized columnar store; here for trade history and reporting, separate from the hot path)*.

> 🎙️ Script: "Money lives in strongly-consistent SQL — funds and holdings ledgers, sharded by user, with real ACID transactions because we can't double-spend. Orders and trades are append-only immutable logs for durability and audit, indexed into a query DB for lookups. The order book itself lives in memory in the engine and is rebuilt by replaying the log on restart. And last prices live in Redis — ephemeral and eventually consistent because being a beat stale on a price is harmless."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "money path = strong + ordered + durable, read path = fan-out + eventual" and reason forward from there.

### How do we match orders correctly, fast, and without double-execution?

**#### Bad: stateless matching workers hitting a shared SQL order book**
**Approach** — N workers read open orders from a SQL table, match, write trades.
**Challenges** — race conditions: two workers match the same resting order → **double-execution** (we sell 100 shares twice). Locking the book per match serializes everything and tanks throughput. ⚠️ **Trap:** treating the order book as ordinary CRUD data — under the open spike this both corrupts and crawls.

**#### Good: single-writer in-memory engine per symbol, fed by a log**
**Approach** — One engine instance owns each symbol's book entirely in memory; it consumes a **per-symbol-partitioned Kafka log** so events arrive in a total order. Match with **price-time priority**. Emit trades to a trade log. No locks — one thread, one symbol.
**Challenges** — if the engine crashes, the in-memory book is gone, and a single instance is a SPOF.

**#### Great: single-writer + log replay + hot-standby replicas**
**Approach** — The log *is* the source of truth, so on crash a standby replays the log from the last snapshot and rebuilds the exact book — deterministic because input order is fixed. Run a **hot standby** consuming the same partition; on failure it's already warm and takes over (leader election via the consumer group / a coordination service). Periodic **snapshots** bound replay time. Symbols are sharded across many engine instances, so we scale horizontally and a hot symbol (RELIANCE at open) gets its own dedicated instance.
**Trade-offs/why real systems do this** — this is exactly the LMAX/exchange pattern: determinism + an ordered log gives you correctness *and* microsecond matching *and* free audit/replay. Cost: failover takes seconds (replay), so we keep snapshots frequent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OQ[[Order log partition=symbol]] -->|ordered| L[Leader engine RELIANCE]
  OQ -->|same stream| S[Hot standby RELIANCE]
  L -->|snapshot| SNAP[(Snapshot store)]
  S -.->|replay on failover| SNAP
  L -->|trades| TQ[[Trade log]]
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class OQ,L,S,SNAP,TQ cold;
```

**Bottleneck:** a single engine thread does maybe ~1M matches/s — way above our 13K/s peak even for one hot symbol, so the binding limit is actually log throughput and settlement, not matching. New ceiling: Kafka partition ~ hundreds of MB/s, fine.

> 🎙️ Script: "I make matching a single-writer, in-memory engine per symbol, fed by an ordered durable log. One thread per symbol means no locks and no double-execution — order is decided by the log. Because matching is deterministic on that input order, I can crash and rebuild the exact book by replaying from a snapshot, and I keep a hot standby already consuming the same stream for fast failover. That's the LMAX/exchange pattern — correctness, microsecond speed, and a free audit trail all at once."

> 🧠 If they ask "why not just lock rows in SQL?": "Locking serializes the whole book and still risks double-fills under contention. Single-writer-per-symbol removes the race by construction — there's only one writer, so there's nothing to lock."

### How do we fan out 7.5M ticks/sec to millions of clients?

**#### Bad: clients poll GET /price every second**
**Approach** — clients poll. **Challenges** — 1.5M clients × 1/s = 1.5M QPS of HTTP overhead, terrible latency, and still stale. Dead on arrival.

**#### Good: flat pub/sub — every client subscribes to a topic**
**Approach** — Redis/Kafka pub/sub per symbol; clients subscribe. **Challenges** — a single broker node caps ~100K pushes/s; 7.5M needs a coordinated tree, and one popular symbol concentrates load on one node (hot key).

**#### Great: tiered broadcast tree + conflation + edge gateways**
**Approach** — (1) **Conflate** at the source: collapse N ticks/sec per symbol to a fixed publish rate (say 4-10/s) — humans can't see more. That alone cuts raw volume massively. (2) A **broadcast tree**: market-data service → regional relays → edge WebSocket gateways, each gateway owning ~50-100K connections and subscribing upstream *once* per symbol regardless of how many of its clients want it (de-duplicated fan-out). (3) New subscribers get the current value instantly from **Redis last-price** so they don't wait for the next tick.
**Trade-offs** — eventual consistency (a few hundred ms stale, acceptable); hot symbols still concentrate at the relay layer but the tree spreads the multiplication across many edge nodes instead of one.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  ME[Engine/Exchange feed] --> MD[Market-data svc - conflate]
  MD --> RT[(Redis last price)]
  MD --> R1[Relay region A]
  MD --> R2[Relay region B]
  R1 --> E1[Edge 50k conns]
  R1 --> E2[Edge 50k conns]
  R2 --> E3[Edge 50k conns]
  E1 --> CL[Clients]
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class ME,MD,RT,R1,R2,E1,E2,E3,CL hot;
```

**Math:** 1.5M conns ÷ 50K per edge = **~30 edge nodes**. Each symbol is subscribed once per edge node, so upstream from edges = 30 × 5000 symbols × ~5/s ≈ 750K msgs/s spread across relays — manageable, vs 7.5M flat.

> 🎙️ Script: "The trick is don't actually send 7.5 million messages. First I conflate — a price that ticks fifty times a second gets published a handful of times, because a human can't see more. Then I build a broadcast tree: the market-data service feeds regional relays which feed edge WebSocket gateways, each owning maybe fifty thousand connections and subscribing to each symbol just once no matter how many of its users want it. New subscribers get the latest price straight from Redis so they're not waiting. That turns a flat 7.5 million into a few hundred thousand spread across thirty-ish edge nodes."

> 🧠 If they ask "why WebSocket not Server-Sent-Events or polling?": "We need server-push at low latency and the client also sends subscribe messages, so a bidirectional WebSocket fits; SSE is one-way and polling is too slow and wasteful at this fan-out."

### How do we guarantee funds aren't double-spent (idempotency + reservation)?

**#### Great: reserve-on-accept + idempotency key + ACID ledger**
**Approach** — Before accepting an order we run an ACID transaction: `available -= cost; reserved += cost` with an optimistic **version** check. The **Idempotency-Key** is stored uniquely; a retry returns the original orderId instead of re-reserving. On fill, settlement converts reserved→spent; on cancel/reject, reserved→available. Every step is a ledger event.
**Read-your-own-writes:** after the 202, the client's funds query reads from the **primary** (or carries a version token) so the user always sees the freshly-blocked balance, never a lagging replica.
**Namespace collision:** orderIds are generated server-side (Snowflake-style: timestamp+shard+seq), so no two gateways can mint the same id; the idempotency key dedups client retries.

> 🧠 If they ask "what if settlement double-processes a trade?": "Each trade has a unique id and settlement is idempotent — applying the same tradeId twice is a no-op via a processed-set with the ledger update in one transaction."

> 🎙️ Script: "Money is never spent twice because I reserve cash in an ACID transaction at accept-time, with a version check, and an idempotency key so a retried tap returns the same order instead of double-reserving. The fill just converts reserved to spent; a cancel returns it. And right after placing, the user's balance read goes to the primary so they always see their own blocked funds immediately."

### Global coordinators
- **Per-symbol log partition leader** — ceiling: one writer; failover via consumer-group leader election + log replay (seconds). If down, that symbol halts (correct — better than mismatching).
- **Funds ledger shard primary** — ACID writes; multi-AZ sync replica, failover promotes replica (RPO 0, RTO seconds).
- **Snowflake ID generator** — per-node, no central coordinator, so no SPOF.

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

- **Availability:** order gateway 99.99% (four 9s) during market hours; market-data 99.9%.
- **Graceful degradation:** if market-data fails → order path still works (you can trade blind, prices freeze with a "stale" banner). If matching for a symbol fails → reject new orders on *that symbol* only, others trade. If Redis last-price down → new subscribers wait for next live tick.
- **RPO/RTO:** order & trade logs and funds ledger = **RPO 0** (synchronous replication, multi-AZ), RTO seconds (replay + promote). Market-data/Redis = RPO doesn't matter (ephemeral), rebuild from bus. Tested restore: nightly replay of the order log into a staging book and reconcile against settlement.
- **Multi-AZ vs region:** synchronous across AZs in one region for the money path (latency-sensitive matching can't afford cross-region sync); async DR replica in a second region with documented failover for disaster.
- **Cost driver:** the dominant cost is the **edge WebSocket fleet + egress bandwidth** for market data (millions of persistent connections), not the DB. Rough order: tens of edge nodes + bandwidth ≫ the modest order DB. I'd ballpark the market-data tier as the majority of monthly infra spend.
- **Ops:** SLO burn-rate alerts on order-ack p99 and fill latency, distributed tracing order→trade→settlement, runbooks for "symbol engine down" and "exchange feed gap."

> 🎙️ Script: "I run the money path synchronously across availability zones with zero data loss and seconds to fail over by replaying the log. The market-data path is allowed to fail soft — prices freeze with a stale banner but you can still trade. The biggest cost isn't the database, it's holding millions of WebSocket connections and the egress to push prices, so that's where I'd optimize spend with conflation and efficient binary framing."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **In-house matching vs route-to-exchange.** I designed an owned engine for depth, but a real Indian broker routes to NSE/BSE. Gave up: actual liquidity/regulatory reality. Reverses if: we *are* the exchange or run an internal dark pool. I'd confirm with the interviewer which world we're in.
- **Conflation of market data.** Sacrifices tick-by-tick fidelity for ~500x less fan-out. Reverses for: algo/HFT clients who need every tick — they'd get a separate raw, co-located feed, not the retail WebSocket.
- **Strong consistency on the whole money path.** Costs latency and caps us to single-region sync. Reverses only if: we accept eventual settlement (we won't — it's money).

> 🎙️ Script: "The two calls I'd most want to confirm: whether we own matching or route to the exchange, since that reshapes the core; and conflation, which is perfect for retail but wrong for algo traders who'd need a separate raw feed. The one thing I won't bend is strong consistency on funds — it's money, eventual isn't an option."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. How do you guarantee order fairness?**
Per-symbol orders go into a single Kafka partition, giving a total order, and the engine matches strictly price-time priority. Because one thread owns the symbol, there's no race to reorder. The log timestamp is the tiebreaker and it's auditable.
> 💬 "One partition per symbol gives total ordering; price-time priority on a single writer means fairness is guaranteed by construction."

**2. What happens at market open when 13K orders/sec hit?**
Orders are absorbed by the durable log instantly (Kafka handles far more), so we never drop them; the engine drains at its own pace and acks are 202 so users aren't blocked. Hot symbols get dedicated engine instances pre-provisioned for open. Risk checks are the likely bottleneck, so we pre-warm funds-ledger connections and cache balances.
> 💬 "The log is a shock absorber — we accept everything durably and match as fast as we can; nobody's order is lost."

**3. How do you prevent a user spending the same money twice?**
An ACID transaction reserves funds at accept-time with an optimistic version check before the order is logged. The idempotency key dedups retries. Fills convert reserved to spent atomically.
> 💬 "We block the cash before we accept the order, in one ACID transaction — there's no window to double-spend."

**4. Engine crashes mid-session — what happens?**
The in-memory book is reconstructable: a hot standby replays from the last snapshot plus subsequent log events, deterministically rebuilding the identical book, then takes over leadership. In-flight orders aren't lost because they're in the log, not in the engine.
> 💬 "Nothing's lost — the log is truth, the standby replays it and rebuilds the exact book in seconds."

**5. How do you stream to millions without melting?**
Conflate at source, then fan out through a tree of relays and edge gateways where each gateway subscribes once per symbol. New clients seed from Redis. That converts millions of raw ticks into a few hundred K of de-duplicated pushes.
> 💬 "I don't send every tick to everyone — I conflate and fan out through a tree, subscribing once per symbol per edge."

**6. Strong vs eventual consistency — where each?**
Funds, holdings, order state = strong (ACID, read-from-primary after write). Market data, last price = eventual (Redis, a few hundred ms stale is fine). The split is by whether staleness can cause financial harm.
> 💬 "Money is strong, prices are eventual — I draw the line at financial harm."

**7. How do you handle a stop-loss order?**
Stop orders rest in a separate trigger store keyed by symbol; the market-data stream feeds a trigger evaluator that, when last price crosses the stop, injects a market/limit order into the normal log. So it reuses the whole pipeline — only the trigger is new.
> 💬 "A stop-loss is a parked trigger; when price crosses, we inject a normal order into the same flow."

**8. What if the exchange feed has a gap or goes down?**
We detect via sequence-number gaps in the feed, mark affected symbols stale, halt new orders on them, and request a snapshot/recovery feed from the exchange. We never match on stale data.
> 💬 "Sequence gaps trigger a halt-and-resync on those symbols — better to pause than trade on bad prices."

**9. How do you audit / replay for regulators?**
Every order and trade is an immutable, ordered, timestamped log event retained for the mandated period; we can replay any window deterministically to reproduce exact state. The query DB is just a materialized view of the log.
> 💬 "The log is the immutable system of record — we can replay any moment exactly for SEBI."

**10. How do you shard, and what's the hot-shard risk?**
Engine sharded by symbol, ledgers by userId. Hot risk is a single popular symbol at open — mitigated by giving high-volume symbols dedicated engine instances and ensuring matching throughput (~1M/s) dwarfs even spiked single-symbol load.
> 💬 "Shard by symbol for matching, by user for money; hot symbols get their own dedicated engine."

**11. Why 202 and not 200 on order placement?**
Matching is asynchronous, so a synchronous 200 with a fill would force us to block the request on the engine and couple the gateway to matching latency. 202 means accepted-and-durable; the fill arrives on the WebSocket.
> 💬 "202 says 'I've durably got your order,' not 'it filled' — fills come async on the socket."

**12. How would you add charting / historical candles later?**
Tap the trade and tick logs into a time-series/OLAP store like ClickHouse, pre-aggregate into 1m/5m/1d candles, serve via a separate read API and CDN. It's a downstream consumer — zero impact on the hot paths.
> 💬 "Charts are just another consumer of the same logs into a time-series DB — fully decoupled."

**13. Network drops mid-WebSocket — does the user miss a fill?**
Order updates are also persisted; on reconnect the client fetches order/fill state via GET and resubscribes, so the WebSocket is a fast-path, not the only path. No fill is ever known only on the wire.
> 💬 "The socket is a convenience — fills are durable, so on reconnect we just re-fetch and re-sync."

**14. Multi-region active-active for the money path?**
No — the money path is single-write-region with synchronous multi-AZ replication, because cross-region sync would add tens of ms to matching and risk split-brain on the ledger. Second region is warm DR with async replication and a documented failover.
> 💬 "Money stays single-region synchronous; cross-region is DR, not active-active — split-brain on a ledger is unacceptable."

---

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is really two systems. The money path: orders come into a gateway, we reserve funds in an ACID transaction, append the order to a durable per-symbol ordered log, and ack with a 202. A single-writer in-memory matching engine per symbol consumes that log, matches with price-time priority or routes to the exchange, and emits trades that settlement turns into real cash and share movements — all strongly consistent and fully replayable for audit, with a hot standby that rebuilds the book from the log on failure. The read path is a 500-to-1 fan-out problem: 7.5 million price ticks a second to a million-plus screens. I solve it by conflating ticks at the source and fanning out through a tree of relays and edge WebSocket gateways, each subscribing once per symbol, with Redis serving the latest price to new subscribers. Money is strong and single-region synchronous; prices are eventual and degrade gracefully. The dominant cost is the WebSocket edge fleet, not the database."
